# JOS-S27: DECISION-LOG.md Doc Standard

## What a DECISION-LOG.md is

A doc capturing **dated entries of decisions** made within a system
or subsystem that don't warrant promotion to a venture-level or
JOS-level decision registry. Append-only, chronological, internal-scope.

DECISION-LOG.md answers: **"What internal choices did this system
make, when, and why?"**

It is NOT the canonical decision registry — that's the per-file
pattern (`decisions/<PREFIX>-D####-<slug>.md`). DECISION-LOG.md is
the lightweight alternative for choices that don't deserve their own
file or their own ID.

## THE TRIGGER RULE (which pattern to use)

JOS supports both patterns deliberately. Use the right one per the
following rule — strict, no judgment calls:

**Use per-file pattern (`decisions/<PREFIX>-D####-<slug>.md`) when
ANY of these is true:**

- The decision affects more than one system or subsystem (cross-cutting)
- The decision warrants promotion to the venture-level (or JOS-level) decision registry
- You will cite the decision by ID from outside the originating system
- The decision is strategic (architecture / governance / lifecycle / scope)
- The decision needs to be reviewed by another stakeholder before locking

**Use DECISION-LOG.md (single file with dated entries) when ALL of these are true:**

- The decision is purely internal to one system or subsystem
- The decision affects only that system's implementation
- You will not cite the decision by ID across the fleet
- The decision is tactical (e.g., "we chose Postgres over Mongo for this subsystem's persistence")
- The decision is local enough that the system's owner can lock it alone

**Trigger heuristic in one sentence:**
> If anyone outside this system would ever reference this decision by ID — **per-file**. If not — **DECISION-LOG.md entry**.

The rule is binary. If you find yourself rationalizing toward one
pattern, default to per-file (over-promoting a tactical choice is
cheap; under-promoting a strategic one is expensive).

## Position in the hierarchy

DECISION-LOG.md **co-elaborates the Lifecycle Contract slot** of
JOS-FW-001's Boundary Contract framework (per JOS-D0054), alongside
VISION.md (forward), ROADMAP.md (path), and CHANGELOG.md (recent changes).

```
Lifecycle Contract Slot
├── VISION.md          where going (forward)
├── ROADMAP.md         coarse strategic path
├── CHANGELOG.md       recent changes (version history)
└── DECISION-LOG.md ←  past internal choices (this standard)
```

## Required sections

### § Overview (1-2 paragraphs)

Identity: what this DECISION-LOG covers, who can append, and the
scope cutoff (i.e., "decisions that affect only this system; if
broader, use venture-D## per-file pattern").

### § Entries (newest at top)

The core. Each entry has:

- **Header:** `## DL-NNNN — <one-line title>` (sequential numbering;
  DL- prefix to disambiguate from per-file D#### IDs)
- **Date:** YYYY-MM-DD
- **Decided by:** name or role
- **Context:** what situation prompted the choice (1-2 sentences)
- **Decision:** what was chosen (one sentence)
- **Rationale:** why (2-4 sentences max — DECISION-LOG entries are
  lightweight; for heavier rationale, use per-file)
- **Consequences:** what changes downstream (1-3 bullets)
- **Status:** active / superseded by DL-NNNN / superseded by venture-D####

Append at top (newest first). Never edit existing entries; supersede
via status flag and new entry.

### § Last appended

Date of most recent entry. Operators scan this to know freshness.

## Suggested sections (optional)

- **Promotion notes** — entries that were considered for promotion to
  per-file and why they weren't (or were)
- **Index** — for long logs, a top-of-file index DL-NNNN → title for
  quick scanning

## What does NOT belong in DECISION-LOG

- **Strategic decisions** → per-file at `decisions/<PREFIX>-D####-<slug>.md`
  (see trigger rule above)
- **Cross-system decisions** → per-file
- **Decisions someone else will reference** → per-file
- **Roadmap items** → ROADMAP.md
- **Recent code changes** → CHANGELOG.md
- **Forward plans** → VISION.md
- **Editable working notes** — DECISION-LOG is append-only; superseded entries stay visible

If you're rewriting an entry, you're doing it wrong. Append a new
entry that supersedes the old.

## Frontmatter schema

```yaml
---
doc_type: decision-log                       # REQUIRED
elaborates_slot: lifecycle                    # REQUIRED — fixed for DECISION-LOG.md
declared_thing: <object_id_or_path>          # REQUIRED — usually a system or subsystem
declared_thing_kind: <type>                  # REQUIRED — typically system | subsystem
owner: <name_or_team>                        # REQUIRED
status: active                               # REQUIRED
created: YYYY-MM-DD                          # REQUIRED
last_modified: YYYY-MM-DD                    # REQUIRED — updated on every append
last_reviewed: YYYY-MM-DD                    # REQUIRED — periodic review for promotion candidates
review_cadence: 90                           # REQUIRED — days
audience: [system-owner, debugger, archaeologist]  # SUGGESTED
diataxis_type: reference                     # FIXED — historical reference
---
```

## Length target

**Grows over time.** No fixed length. Entries accumulate; doc gets
longer as the system's history accumulates.

- A new system: DECISION-LOG starts ~30 lines (frontmatter +
  overview + 1-2 entries)
- A mature system: may be 500+ lines (years of internal decisions)
- A DECISION-LOG with 0 entries beyond stub is a smell — either
  the system isn't making decisions (unlikely) or decisions are
  going unrecorded

## What makes a *good* DECISION-LOG.md

1. **Append-only.** Existing entries never edited; supersession via
   new entry with status flag.
2. **Newest at top.** Readers care about recent decisions; old context
   is archaeology.
3. **Trigger rule respected.** Strategic / cross-system / cite-able
   decisions promote to per-file; only the local-tactical lives here.
4. **Concise per-entry.** 2-4 sentence rationale max. If you need more,
   it's per-file material.
5. **Periodic promotion review.** During `last_reviewed`, walk the
   log and check whether any tactical entries have grown into
   strategic-feeling territory (promote them).

## Anti-patterns

| Anti-pattern | Symptom | Fix |
|--------------|---------|-----|
| Strategic decisions in DECISION-LOG | A cross-system choice or architecture-level decision logged here | Promote to per-file; leave a referring stub in DECISION-LOG |
| Editing existing entries | Backwards-rewriting of historical entries | Supersede with new entry; never edit |
| Empty DECISION-LOG | File exists but only 0-2 stub entries despite system being mature | Either the system isn't making real decisions OR decisions are going unrecorded — investigate |
| Both patterns for same decision | Decision lives both per-file AND in DECISION-LOG | Pick per-file; remove from DECISION-LOG (or vice-versa) |
| Bypassing per-file for cite-ability | "I want to cite this elsewhere but it's only logged" | Promote to per-file first; THEN cite |
| Verbose entries | 2-page entry in DECISION-LOG | That's per-file material — promote |

## How to extract YOUR DECISION-LOG.md

DECISION-LOG entries are extracted **at the moment of decision**, not
backfilled later. Backfilled decision logs are archaeology, not record.

**Source material** (in order of authority):
1. The chat / pairing session where the choice happened — capture
   while the rationale is fresh
2. PR descriptions that explain a non-obvious choice — promote them
   into log entries
3. Code review discussions where a non-trivial tradeoff was named
4. Standup notes where a direction was set
5. NOT post-hoc rationalization — if you can't remember why, log it
   as "rationale lost; preserved for archaeology" rather than inventing

**Extraction procedure:**
1. As soon as a decision lands, ask the trigger question: "Will anyone
   outside this system cite this by ID?" If yes → per-file decision
   (decisions/PREFIX-D####). If no → DECISION-LOG entry.
2. Author the entry IN THE SAME PR as the change that implements the
   decision. Don't let drift open.
3. Use the canonical 7-field block (Date, Decided by, Context, Decision,
   Rationale, Consequences, Status).
4. Insert at TOP. Never sort, never edit existing entries.
5. If superseding an earlier entry, write a NEW entry with `Status:
   supersedes DL-NNNN` and update the old entry's status to
   `superseded by DL-MMMM`.

**When in the lifecycle:** start the DECISION-LOG the moment the
system is named. Add entries as decisions happen — same-day discipline.
Periodically (quarterly) review for promotion candidates.

## How to write it well

- **Voice:** terse, factual, past-tense. "Chose Postgres over MySQL
  because we already operate Postgres in the fleet." Not "we decided
  to go with..."
- **One decision, one entry.** No bundling. Even if two choices were
  made same day.
- **Rationale in 2-4 sentences max.** If you need more, it's per-file
  material — promote it.
- **Consequences are concrete.** "Forces operators to learn pgvector
  syntax" — not "may have downstream effects."
- **Append-only discipline is sacred.** Editing existing entries
  invalidates every reader's mental model and breaks supersession
  chains.

**The honest test:** an archaeologist six months from now can read
the log top-to-bottom and reconstruct the system's evolution without
calling anyone. If they need to ask "wait, why did we do X?", an
entry is missing or too thin.

## Validation rules

**Mechanically checkable:**

- File present at system/subsystem root (when applicable)
- Frontmatter matches schema; `elaborates_slot: lifecycle`
- Entries follow numbered DL-NNNN format
- Each entry has all required fields (Date, Decided by, Context,
  Decision, Rationale, Consequences, Status)
- `last_modified` matches the date of the most recent entry
- No entry status references a non-existent DL-NNNN or D#### (cross-ref resolution)

**Human judgment:**

- Whether the trigger rule was respected (no strategic decisions in DECISION-LOG)
- Whether entries are concise enough to belong here
- Whether the periodic-promotion review caught everything

## Embedded explainer (top of every DECISION-LOG.md)

```markdown
<!--
This is a DECISION-LOG.md. It captures dated, append-only entries of
LOCAL TACTICAL decisions made within this system or subsystem.

TRIGGER RULE — use per-file pattern (decisions/<PREFIX>-D####-<slug>.md)
INSTEAD when ANY of these is true:
  - Decision affects more than this one system (cross-cutting)
  - Decision warrants promotion to venture-level or JOS-level registry
  - You will cite the decision by ID from outside this system
  - Decision is strategic (architecture / governance / lifecycle / scope)

If ALL of these are true, use DECISION-LOG.md entry HERE:
  - Decision is purely internal to this system
  - Decision affects only this system's implementation
  - You will not cite the decision by ID across the fleet
  - Decision is tactical

One-sentence heuristic: "If anyone outside this system would ever
reference this decision by ID — per-file. If not — DECISION-LOG entry."

Append at TOP (newest first). Never edit existing entries; supersede
via status flag + new entry.

Standard: JOS-S27 (standards/doc-types/JOS-S27-decision-log-doc-standard.md)
Boundary Contract slot: Lifecycle (per JOS-D0054)
-->
```

## Related governance

- **JOS-D0054** Doc-Types Elaborate Boundary Contract Slots
- **JOS-SOP-001** Creating a New Governance Object (for per-file pattern)
- **JOS-SO-005** Registry Is Structural Truth (per-file decisions register; DECISION-LOG entries do not)
- **JOS-P24** Declared, Exemplified, Guided, Governed
- **JOS-METHOD-DOC-TYPE-STANDARDIZATION** Doc-Type Standardization Method (formerly JOS-METHOD-001, UUID preserved per JOS-SOP-014)

## Canonical examples

JOS uses **per-file pattern** at venture-root level. See:
- `decisions/` directory (53+ JOS-D#### files)
- Most-recent example: `decisions/JOS-D0054-doc-types-elaborate-boundary-contract-slots.md`

Foundry uses **per-system DECISION-LOG.md** pattern for system-internal
tactical decisions. See `Foundry-Agent-System/docs/DECISION-LOG.md` +
per-subsystem instances.

Both are correct; the trigger rule determines which.
